items.md: This revision has been brought out as a major exercise to reflect the improvement in knowledge and understanding over the last five decades. This exercise has been motivated and expedited by the growing threat of malevolent and accidental blast loading of structures in the country. Structures due to explosions are subjected to rapidly moving shock wave which may exert very high pressures, though, the peak intensity lasts for a very small duration only. The blast wave loads the exposed surface of the structure and then the load is transmitted to the other elements. Thus, the response assessment of each individual structural element is of prime importance in blast-resistant design of structures.

items.md: This standard provides a minimum design blast threat that can be adopted in absence of threat assessment. However, the blast threat specified by the standard can be superseded by the values informed through actual threat assessment. The threat assessment shall be initiated by the owner and conducted by qualified personnel to determine the explosive charge mass and standoff distances. The necessity of blast-resistant design for a structure shall be specified by the owner of the facility based on threat assessment and risk analysis. This standard does not provide the methodology for risk analysis. Reference to a particular type of structure in this standard does not automatically requires it to be designed for explosion threats.

items.md: The application of blast load estimation charts and the structural analysis methods provided in the standard depends on the scaled distance of the explosive charge mass. In general, the provisions of this standard are not applicable for scaled distances smaller than 0.4 m/kg<sup>1/3</sup>; and secondary post-blast consequences such as fragmentation, fire, and progressive or disproportionate collapse are not included in this standard.

items.md: ## PART 1 ABOVE-GROUND EXPLOSIONS
items.value: PART 1 ABOVE-GROUND EXPLOSIONS

items.md: ( *First Revision* )
items.value: ( First Revision )

items.md: ### 1 SCOPE
items.value: 1 SCOPE

items.md: **1.1** This standard (Part 1) prescribes the minimum criteria for analysis and design of new structures for blast effects of above-ground explosions of high-explosives under three different detonation scenarios: surface burst, free-air burst, and air burst. The primary objective of this standard is to minimize human casualties and safeguard assets due to above ground explosions. The performance expectation of a structure designed as per the provisions of this standard would depend on the type of protection category selected for that structure and the blast loading determined through threat assessment.
items.value: 1.1 This standard (Part 1) prescribes the minimum criteria for analysis and design of new structures for blast effects of above-ground explosions of high-explosives under three different detonation scenarios: surface burst, free-air burst, and air burst. The primary objective of this standard is to minimize human casualties and safeguard assets due to above ground explosions. The performance expectation of a structure designed as per the provisions of this standard would depend on the type of protection category selected for that structure and the blast loading determined through threat assessment.

items.md: **1.2** This standard shall be used in conjunction with other applicable standards for analysis and design of structures. This standard does not supersede or replace other design standards. This standard shall be applicable if design against malevolent or accidental blast loading is determined to be a credible threat to a structure.
items.value: 1.2 This standard shall be used in conjunction with other applicable standards for analysis and design of structures. This standard does not supersede or replace other design standards. This standard shall be applicable if design against malevolent or accidental blast loading is determined to be a credible threat to a structure.

items.md: **1.3** The owner of a structure is the competent authority who can determine the necessity of blast-resistant design. In the scenario of accidental and malevolent blast event which may pose public health and safety issue, competent authorities other than the owner of building that govern or regulate the construction, operation, and maintenance of the structural facility, can specify the requirement of blast-resistant design.
items.value: 1.3 The owner of a structure is the competent authority who can determine the necessity of blast-resistant design. In the scenario of accidental and malevolent blast event which may pose public health and safety issue, competent authorities other than the owner of building that govern or regulate the construction, operation, and maintenance of the structural facility, can specify the requirement of blast-resistant design.

items.md: **1.4** The provisions of this standard do not address the following:

items.md: a) Estimation of structural loads due to nuclear detonations, pressure vessel explosions, dust, and vapour cloud explosions;
items.value: a) Estimation of structural loads due to nuclear detonations, pressure vessel explosions, dust, and vapour cloud explosions;

items.md: b) Effects of cased explosives and shaped charges;
items.value: b) Effects of cased explosives and shaped charges;

items.md: c) Threats emanating from fragmentation, induced fire or thermal effects, biological or chemical radiations, electromagnetic pulse (EMP) and deflagration events; and
items.value: c) Threats emanating from fragmentation, induced fire or thermal effects, biological or chemical radiations, electromagnetic pulse (EMP) and deflagration events; and

items.md: d) Detonations in contact with structural elements.

items.md: **3.5 Decay Parameter** — The coefficient of the negative power of exponent $e$ governing the fall of pressure with time in the pressure-time curves.
items.value: 3.5 Decay Parameter — The coefficient of the negative power of exponent $e$ governing the fall of pressure with time in the pressure-time curves.

items.md: **3.6 Detonation** — Fast and sudden release of huge amount of energy through a stable chemical reaction in which reaction front propagates into unreacted substance at supersonic speed resulting in formation of shock waves.
items.value: 3.6 Detonation — Fast and sudden release of huge amount of energy through a stable chemical reaction in which reaction front propagates into unreacted substance at supersonic speed resulting in formation of shock waves.

items.md: **3.7 Drag Force** — Force on a structure or structural element due to the dynamic pressure of the blast wave. On any structural element, the drag force equals dynamic pressure multiplied by the drag coefficient and the area of the element.
items.value: 3.7 Drag Force — Force on a structure or structural element due to the dynamic pressure of the blast wave. On any structural element, the drag force equals dynamic pressure multiplied by the drag coefficient and the area of the element.

items.md: **3.8 Ductility Ratio** — Ratio of the maximum deflection to the deflection corresponding to the elastic limit.
items.value: 3.8 Ductility Ratio — Ratio of the maximum deflection to the deflection corresponding to the elastic limit.

items.md: **3.9 Dynamic Increase Factor (DIF)** — A factor applied to material strength to account for high strain rate loading effects.
items.value: 3.9 Dynamic Increase Factor (DIF) — A factor applied to material strength to account for high strain rate loading effects.

items.md: **3.10 Dynamic Pressure** — The pressure due to air mass movement behind the shock front.
items.value: 3.10 Dynamic Pressure — The pressure due to air mass movement behind the shock front.

items.md: **3.11 Exponential Blast Pressure History** — The variation of blast pressure with time at a point represented through an exponential decaying function, which considers positive and negative phase of blast overpressures.
items.value: 3.11 Exponential Blast Pressure History — The variation of blast pressure with time at a point represented through an exponential decaying function, which considers positive and negative phase of blast overpressures.

items.md: **3.12 Far-field Detonation** — Detonation at a scaled distance ($Z$) above which the resulting blast pressure distribution is uniform over the reflecting surface of element being considered. It corresponds to a value of $Z$ greater than 1.2 m/kg<sup>1/3</sup>.
items.value: 3.12 Far-field Detonation — Detonation at a scaled distance ($Z$) above which the resulting blast pressure distribution is uniform over the reflecting surface of element being considered. It corresponds to a value of $Z$ greater than 1.2 m/kg<sup>1/3</sup>.

items.md: **3.13 High-Explosives** — Chemical explosive that undergoes detonation to produce shock waves with high pressure and short duration.
items.value: 3.13 High-Explosives — Chemical explosive that undergoes detonation to produce shock waves with high pressure and short duration.

items.md: **3.14 Impulse** — The integral of the pressure-time curve per unit projected area due to explosion (see **3.27**).
items.value: 3.14 Impulse — The integral of the pressure-time curve per unit projected area due to explosion (see 3.27).

items.md: **3.15 Mach Number** — The ratio of the speed of the shock front propagation to the speed of sound in standard atmosphere at sea level.
items.value: 3.15 Mach Number — The ratio of the speed of the shock front propagation to the speed of sound in standard atmosphere at sea level.

items.md: **3.16 Negative Phase** — The duration of reflected overpressure when it is below the ambient atmospheric pressure.
items.value: 3.16 Negative Phase — The duration of reflected overpressure when it is below the ambient atmospheric pressure.

items.md: **3.17 Overpressure** — The rise in pressure above atmospheric pressure due to the shock wave from an air blast.
items.value: 3.17 Overpressure — The rise in pressure above atmospheric pressure due to the shock wave from an air blast.

items.md: **3.18 Positive Phase** — The duration of overpressure when it is above the ambient atmospheric pressure.
items.value: 3.18 Positive Phase — The duration of overpressure when it is above the ambient atmospheric pressure.

items.md: **3.19 Protection Category** — Category assigned to different structures that correlates its importance to the performance goals through qualitative estimate of damage.
items.value: 3.19 Protection Category — Category assigned to different structures that correlates its importance to the performance goals through qualitative estimate of damage.

items.md: **3.20 Reflected Overpressure** — The overpressure resulting due to reflection of a shock wave front striking any surface. If the shock front is parallel to the surface, the reflection is normal.
items.value: 3.20 Reflected Overpressure — The overpressure resulting due to reflection of a shock wave front striking any surface. If the shock front is parallel to the surface, the reflection is normal.

items.md: **3.21 Regular Shaped Structure** — A structure that does not have any spatial geometric irregularity.
items.value: 3.21 Regular Shaped Structure — A structure that does not have any spatial geometric irregularity.

items.md: **3.22 Scaled Distance** — The distance between the centre of explosion to the point of interest divided by the cube root of the charge mass expressed in equivalent TNT. The unit for scaled distance is m/kg<sup>1/3</sup>.
items.value: 3.22 Scaled Distance — The distance between the centre of explosion to the point of interest divided by the cube root of the charge mass expressed in equivalent TNT. The unit for scaled distance is m/kg<sup>1/3</sup>.

items.md: **3.23 Shape Function** — The displaced shape of the component as a function of its length normalized to the maximum deflection.
items.value: 3.23 Shape Function — The displaced shape of the component as a function of its length normalized to the maximum deflection.

items.md: **3.24 Shock Wave Front** — The discontinuity between the blast wave and the surrounding atmosphere. It propagates away from the point of explosion in all directions at a speed greater than the speed of sound in the undisturbed atmosphere.
items.value: 3.24 Shock Wave Front — The discontinuity between the blast wave and the surrounding atmosphere. It propagates away from the point of explosion in all directions at a speed greater than the speed of sound in the undisturbed atmosphere.

items.md: **3.25 Side-on (Incident) Overpressure** — Overpressure if it is not reflected by any surface.
items.value: 3.25 Side-on (Incident) Overpressure — Overpressure if it is not reflected by any surface.

items.md: **3.26 Simplified Overpressure History** — A triangular representation of the overpressure history obtained by equating the impulse and the peak overpressure of the exponential representation of the overpressure history.
items.value: 3.26 Simplified Overpressure History — A triangular representation of the overpressure history obtained by equating the impulse and the peak overpressure of the exponential representation of the overpressure history.

items.md: **3.27 Specific Impulse** — The area under the pressure-time curve due to explosion. This is often referred to as impulse (see **3.14**).
items.value: 3.27 Specific Impulse — The area under the pressure-time curve due to explosion. This is often referred to as impulse (see 3.14).

items.md: **3.28 Standoff Distance (R)** — The shortest distance between the centre of detonation and the point of interest at which the effects of explosion need to be considered.
items.value: 3.28 Standoff Distance (R) — The shortest distance between the centre of detonation and the point of interest at which the effects of explosion need to be considered.

items.md: **3.29 Support Rotation** — The angle formed by a flexural member in its deflected shape with respect to the chord adjoining the two support ends (see Fig. 22).
items.value: 3.29 Support Rotation — The angle formed by a flexural member in its deflected shape with respect to the chord adjoining the two support ends (see Fig. 22).

items.md: # 5 GENERAL PRINCIPLES
items.value: 5 GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.md: ## 5.1 Attributes of Blast Resistant Design
items.value: 5.1 Attributes of Blast Resistant Design

items.md: The design process of a building against blast should start with planning of building layout and arrangements. The distance between the explosive and the building shall be maximized through a combination of site-access controls, landforms, anti-ram structures, street and site landscaping, and perimeter walls. The building should preferably have short-face oriented towards blast source. Also, it shall be located uphill and placed upstream of wind direction and away from congestion. The structures and their contents that need protection against an explosion event should be decided and all possible scenarios need to be considered which can cause damage or occupant injuries. The overall design of a structure shall aim to incorporate following attributes in the overall planning and design of a facility against explosion threats:

items.md: ## 6.1 Threat Assessment Parameters
items.value: 6.1 Threat Assessment Parameters

items.md: A threat assessment shall be carried out to determine all potential sources of detonation.
items.value: A threat assessment shall be carried out to determine all potential sources of detonation.

items.md: The threat assessment shall identify two parameters for the purpose of calculation of blast load parameters, namely:
items.value: The threat assessment shall identify two parameters for the purpose of calculation of blast load parameters, namely:

items.md:
a) charge mass; and
b) standoff distance.
items.value:
a) charge mass; and
b) standoff distance.

items.md: If threat assessment identifies multiple sources of detonation, the effect of blast on the structure shall be assessed considering individual, as well as combinations of all identified detonation threats.

items.md: # 7 BLAST LOAD
items.value: 7 BLAST LOAD

items.md: **7.1 Blast Wave Propagation**
items.value: 7.1 Blast Wave Propagation

items.md: The blast loads for design of structures and components shall be determined from blast pressure history.
items.value: The blast loads for design of structures and components shall be determined from blast pressure history.

items.md: The variation of pressure at a fixed location away from the centre of detonation shall be obtained using following assumptions:
items.value: The variation of pressure at a fixed location away from the centre of detonation shall be obtained using following assumptions:

items.md: a) The shock wave due to explosion moves away from the centre of detonation at a speed, $U$;
items.value: a) The shock wave due to explosion moves away from the centre of detonation at a speed, $U$;

items.md: b) When the shock wave reaches a point at the arrival time $t\_a$, the pressure rises instantaneously from the ambient pressure ($p\_a$) to a peak pressure. This peak pressure, $p\_{so}$, is known as side-on overpressure or peak incident overpressure;
items.value: b) When the shock wave reaches a point at the arrival time $t_a$, the pressure rises instantaneously from the ambient pressure ($p_a$) to a peak pressure. This peak pressure, $p_{so}$, is known as side-on overpressure or peak incident overpressure;

items.md: c) The time needed for the pressure to reach its peak value is very small and for design purposes it is assumed to be zero;
items.value: c) The time needed for the pressure to reach its peak value is very small and for design purposes it is assumed to be zero;

items.md: d) The overpressure decreases exponentially from its peak value to the ambient pressure over the positive phase duration ($t\_o$). After the positive phase of the pressure-time profile, the pressure becomes smaller than the ambient value (referred to as negative pressure), and finally returns to the ambient pressure, $p\_a$;
items.value: d) The overpressure decreases exponentially from its peak value to the ambient pressure over the positive phase duration ($t_o$). After the positive phase of the pressure-time profile, the pressure becomes smaller than the ambient value (referred to as negative pressure), and finally returns to the ambient pressure, $p_a$;

items.md: e) The movement of air caused by the shock wave induces dynamic pressure, in addition to blast pressure. Air dynamic pressure $q\_o$, and peak particle velocity $u$ shall be obtained using peak incident pressure, $p\_{so}$ (see Fig. 9 and Fig. 10); and
items.value: e) The movement of air caused by the shock wave induces dynamic pressure, in addition to blast pressure. Air dynamic pressure $q_o$, and peak particle velocity $u$ shall be obtained using peak incident pressure, $p_{so}$ (see Fig. 9 and Fig. 10); and

items.md: f) Shock wave upon reflection from a rigid surface would apply reflected overpressure, $p\_r$, to the reflecting surface. The variation of reflected overpressure with time for normal reflection follows the same trend as the incident overpressure but characterized by significantly higher peak overpressure.
items.value: f) Shock wave upon reflection from a rigid surface would apply reflected overpressure, $p_r$, to the reflecting surface. The variation of reflected overpressure with time for normal reflection follows the same trend as the incident overpressure but characterized by significantly higher peak overpressure.

items.md: **7.2 Decay of Blast Overpressure with Time**
items.value: 7.2 Decay of Blast Overpressure with Time

items.md: The blast overpressure is assumed to vary with time, $t$ according to the following exponential relationship (see Fig. 5):
items.value: The blast overpressure is assumed to vary with time, $t$ according to the following exponential relationship (see Fig. 5):

items.md: $$p\_s(t) = p\_o \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t\_o} \right) e^{-b(t/t\_o)}$$
items.value: $$p_s(t) = p_o \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_o} \right) e^{-b(t/t_o)}$$

items.md: where
items.value: where

items.md: $p\_o$ = peak blast overpressure, which is interpreted as the peak incident and reflected overpressures for the incident and the reflected blast wave, respectively; and
items.value: $p_o$ = peak blast overpressure, which is interpreted as the peak incident and reflected overpressures for the incident and the reflected blast wave, respectively; and

items.md: $b$ = decay coefficient; and $t\_o$ is the positive phase duration. Special literature is to be referred for the calculation of the blast wave decay parameter, $b$.
items.value: $b$ = decay coefficient; and $t_o$ is the positive phase duration. Special literature is to be referred for the calculation of the blast wave decay parameter, $b$.

items.md: **7.3 Blast Overpressure History Parameters**
items.value: 7.3 Blast Overpressure History Parameters

items.md: The maximum values of the positive side-on overpressure $p\_{so}$, incident impulse $i\_s$, reflected over pressure $p\_r$, reflected impulse $i\_r$, positive phase duration $t\_o$, shock wave front velocity $U$, and wave length $L\_w$, for varying scaled distances, are to be obtained for a spherical TNT explosion in free air and a hemispherical TNT explosion on the surface using Fig. 7 and Fig. 8, respectively. Further, air dynamic pressure $q\_o$, and peak particle velocity $u$, are to be obtained using Fig. 9 and Fig. 10, respectively.

Further, air dynamic pressure $q_o$, and peak particle velocity $u$, are to be obtained using Fig. 9 and Fig. 10, respectively.

items.md: **7.4 Oblique Reflection of Blast Wave**
items.value: 7.4 Oblique Reflection of Blast Wave

items.md: The clauses of this section are applicable to determine the peak reflected overpressure for scenarios where the angle between the wave front and the interacting surface is $\alpha$ ($\le 90^\circ$) as shown in Fig. 6. In such case, the peak reflected overpressure ($p\_{ro}$) is to be determined as multiplication of peak incident overpressure ($p\_{so}$) with a reflection coefficient ($C\_{r\alpha}$). The reflection coefficient at any angle is to be determined from Fig. 11 for given incident overpressure.
items.value: The clauses of this section are applicable to determine the peak reflected overpressure for scenarios where the angle between the wave front and the interacting surface is $\alpha$ ($\le 90^\circ$) as shown in Fig. 6. In such case, the peak reflected overpressure ($p_{ro}$) is to be determined as multiplication of peak incident overpressure ($p_{so}$) with a reflection coefficient ($C_{r\alpha}$). The reflection coefficient at any angle is to be determined from Fig. 11 for given incident overpressure.

items.md: Fully vented structures are to be primarily subjected to shock pressures and the effect of gas pressures may be neglected. The reflected overpressure history at any point inside the structure is represented in Fig. 14. The peak pressure and impulse for the first pulse may be obtained using the procedure described in 7.3 as a case of direct reflection of the blast wave propagating from the centre of detonation to the point of interest. The second and the third triangular pulse shall be constructed from the characteristics of the first pulse.

items.md: **8.2.1** *Types of Structures*

items.md: The below-ground structures are classified into buried and semi-buried structures depending upon the earth cover and slopes of earth berms. The buried structures are subjected only to the general overpressure $p\_{so}$, the reflected and dynamic pressures being neglected. The semi-buried structures are subjected to partial dynamic pressures besides the general overpressure. Both are acted upon by air-induced ground shock also.

items.md: ### 9.2.2 Equivalent Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) Model
items.value: 9.2.2 Equivalent Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) Model

items.md: The structural element or system may be replaced by an equivalent single spring-mass system having effective stiffness $k\_E$ and effective mass equal to $K\_{LM} M\_t$, where $M\_t$ is the actual mass of the member under consideration and $K\_{LM}$ is a load-mass factor depending upon the stiffness and mass distribution in the member and its boundary conditions. The equivalent system is defined so that the deflection of the equivalent single mass is the same as that of some significant point in the given structure. The effective stiffness $k\_E$ is defined with respect to the deflection of this point.
items.value: The structural element or system may be replaced by an equivalent single spring-mass system having effective stiffness $k_E$ and effective mass equal to $K_{LM} M_t$, where $M_t$ is the actual mass of the member under consideration and $K_{LM}$ is a load-mass factor depending upon the stiffness and mass distribution in the member and its boundary conditions. The equivalent system is defined so that the deflection of the equivalent single mass is the same as that of some significant point in the given structure. The effective stiffness $k_E$ is defined with respect to the deflection of this point.

items.md: The load-mass factor $K\_{LM}$ is equal to the ratio of mass factor $K\_M$ to the load factor $K\_L$. The factors are evaluated on the basis of an assumed deflected shape of the structure as given below:
items.value: The load-mass factor $K_{LM}$ is equal to the ratio of mass factor $K_M$ to the load factor $K_L$. The factors are evaluated on the basis of an assumed deflected shape of the structure as given below:

items.md: $$K\_M = \frac{1}{M\_t} \[\sum\_{r=1}^{n} M\_r \phi\_r^2 + \int m \phi^2(x) , dx]$$
items.value: $$K_M = \frac{1}{M_t} [\sum_{r=1}^{n} M_r \phi_r^2 + \int m \phi^2(x) , dx]$$

items.md: $$K\_L = \frac{1}{P\_t} \[\sum\_{r=1}^{j} p\_r \phi\_r + \int p(x) \phi(x) , dx]$$
items.value: $$K_L = \frac{1}{P_t} [\sum_{r=1}^{j} p_r \phi_r + \int p(x) \phi(x) , dx]$$

items.md: Values of the factors $k\_E$ and $K\_{LM}$ for some structural members are given in Table 17 to Table 19. The effective SDOF parameters for other type of structural members shall be calculated by assuming an appropriate deflected shape as per 9.2.2.2.
items.value: Values of the factors $k_E$ and $K_{LM}$ for some structural members are given in Table 17 to Table 19. The effective SDOF parameters for other type of structural members shall be calculated by assuming an appropriate deflected shape as per 9.2.2.2.

items.md: #### 9.2.2.2 Deflected shape
items.value: 9.2.2.2 Deflected shape

items.md: The deflected shape is suitably chosen to resemble as far as possible the true deflected shape taking into consideration whether the structure or member remains elastic or goes into the plastic range. It may be taken the same as due to static application of the dynamic load on the structure. The deflected shape is normalized such that $\phi(x) = 1$ at the point with respect to which the effective stiffness $k\_E$ is defined.
items.value: The deflected shape is suitably chosen to resemble as far as possible the true deflected shape taking into consideration whether the structure or member remains elastic or goes into the plastic range. It may be taken the same as due to static application of the dynamic load on the structure. The deflected shape is normalized such that $\phi(x) = 1$ at the point with respect to which the effective stiffness $k_E$ is defined.

items.md: #### 9.2.2.3 Effective time-period
items.value: 9.2.2.3 Effective time-period

items.md: The effective time period $T$ of the structural member may be calculated from the equation:
items.value: The effective time period $T$ of the structural member may be calculated from the equation:

items.md: $$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{K\_{LM} M\_t}{k\_E}}$$
items.value: $$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{K_{LM} M_t}{k_E}}$$

* b) A single flexural deformation mode controls the dynamic response of the structural system.

items.md: The methods described in the standard to estimate blast overpressures are independent of the type of structure (for example, buildings, bridges, etc) and shall be applicable provided loading criteria in relevant sections are satisfied. The applicability of an analysis method is governed by the response characteristics (like flexure) of the blast loaded system. The simplified SDOF methods of analysis can be adopted for flexure response evaluation of individual blast loaded member.
